replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Those relate to nonclassical aspects.  In classical astrology (and thus also in traditional geomancy), the opposition, trine, square, sextile, and conjunction were the only aspects that mattered. Johannes Kepler added a bunch of additional aspects, and the two you're talking about (the semisextile of 30° and the inconjunct of 150°) are among them. However, not everyone uses them and geomancy hasn't added them. It would take some serious research into geomantic charts to figure out whether they're significant and, if so, what their significance might be.

replies.title: Re: How to be a healer
replies.content:
Hey Muffin,

You sound a lot like myself, 10 years ago. I am about a year and a half from being able to legally practice homeopathy without supervision in my home country; completed all required coursework, short of graduation by authorship of one peer reviewed article, and still in need to sort out 900hrs of mandatory public service prior to applying for a State license. So please let me share what I've been able to pick up along the journey.

1. Healing can be grubby work at times. You must be at-easy to be around suffering people, people in pain, people not in their prime. It is rewarding to help people back in their feet, but that would be very taxing to do if you are oversensitive. You will hear sad tales, you will see and smell gross things. At times you may need to inquire into matters people wish not to talk about. You not necessarily must like it, but if this affects you so much, you will be miserable at work and eventually bail out.

2. How people perceive you is important. I am still struggling with this particular one. In my previous career it was me and the machine; and the people paying the bills literally did not care if I showed up in pajamas, as long as the machine did what it was meant to do. Today, I find that I must mind what sort of image I... elicit??? in the patient's (or potential patient's) imagination. Just by donning a white coat you are the embodiment of a powerful healing symbol, but you can easily turn this image in their polar opposite. And I am not talking only about being well groomed, though there is that. You need to connect with your patient for your advice to be most effective. It is not a barrier, but you need to be comfortable with this side of the trade as well; for you to thrive instead of cringe.

3. Knowledge of modern medicine will take you a long way. Many alt-healers despise this knowledge, to the detriment of their patients. Even if you know nothing of moderns drugs and surgery... being able to accurately model what's going on inside your patient's body is invaluable. At the least it will help you not to mask troubling symptoms with easy remedies. On a good day it will help you leverage your herbs/energy work/etc and fix illnesses that are not obvious but already there. What I mean to say is that even if you do not complete the 10 year plan, if you end up in health the knowledge you gather along the way will be worth it(and even better, in your case it will be free).

4. That said, you need to find a way to earn a living while you work on your medical degree. Free tuition is just half of the equation, you must find a way to keep you and yours fed, clothed and sheltered while this education lasts. You must do this anyways, so maybe you'll want to think about #1 again and consider: Being a doctor will not fix your money problem in the foreseeable future, and that problem needs fixing sooner rather than later. If you want to be a doctor and help suffering people, go and get a medical education; if you want other people to take care of you because you are their doctor... don't. There are other trades that people value, and whose barriers of entry are way lower.

5. Med education is hard on the brain. I am no-genius-but-above-average intelligence, and well suited to scholarly in general; yet it was no walk in the park to hit those physiology and pathology books. I will not say you cannot be a doctor if you are not super smart, but you will need at least some good study habits and memory skills in order to see it through. If those skills are not already in place today, you may need to remedy that before committing to any hard deadlines (and higher education is a very long sequence of hard urgent deadlines, one after another).

I am genuinely sorry if I come across preachy. That's another issue I am struggling with nowadays.

comments.content: What is an inconjunct aspect and what does it mean? What does it mean when a planet is peregrine?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
An inconjunct is an aspect of 150° between two planets, measured from the location of Earth. It's an aspect of frustration, delay, and denial; while it's normally counted among the minor aspects, I've found in my own practice that it's very often as powerful as a sextile.

A planet is peregrine when it is neither dignified nor debilitated by its placement in the zodiac. Every planet has some signs, and some portions of signs, where it receives dignity, and some signs where it receives debility; when it's in none of these, it's considered peregrine, and weak.

comments.title: Dangers of Neo-Paganism
comments.content:
A question about neo-paganism, if I may. Just how harmful is it to a magical practitioner to get too involved with neo-paganism? In my experience, as soon as one even thinks about identifying as a pagan, one is assaulted with a whole raft of baggage: one is encouraged to hold correct political opinions, to avoid any kind of non-woke behaviour you can think of (cultural appropriation, victim-shaming, transphobia, etc.), and even to hold the correct opinions on the history of witchcraft, the origins of the Celtic myths, which books to read, etc. And if one tries to ignore the above, then the egregore somehow manages to invoke feelings of guilt in oneself, whatever one thinks about it personally, which is a huge red flag as far as I'm concerned.

What would you say is a safe distance from the whole business? Are some parts of it more hazardous than others? I can see paths like devotional polytheism as being relatively safe - I'm just not interested full stop, and I can just happily disagree with even the more opinionated bloggers. But it's the more mainstream areas which seem to be more problematic - 'generic paganism', 'pagan witchcraft', etc. Should one de-paganize one's bookshelf - there are one or two authors in particular I have in mind (not including yourself, of course)? Is it safe attending pagan events (even just for the music)? And however has paganism managed to get a reputation for encouraging people to think for themselves, and reject dogma and doctrine, when (again in my experience) the Anglican Church is far more open and tolerant of dissenting opinion? These days paganism seems to be about as tolerant and open as the Chinese Communist Party.

replies.title: Re: Dangers of Neo-Paganism
replies.content:
As someone who's been in and out of paganism for twenty years now, I think this level of intolerance is pretty new. It's always been left-leaning, but in the past people would readily recommend authors because they were expert in the relevant area, who are now not to be touched with a bargepole,for example Edred Thorsson.

I don't think this intolerance is an essential part of it, and I find it strange that people who are (neo)pagans in the sense used in official classifications like the census have suddenly started to talk about the politics-first left-wing pagans as if they are a separate group altogether, rather than calling them "SJW pagans" or "woke pagans".

replies.title: Re: Dangers of Neo-Paganism
replies.content: The Neopagan scene used to be a very different place. I attended Pantheacon in San Jose, one ofthe largest Pagan events in the country, for a decade; when it started out it was full of divergent ideas, contrasting opinions, and plenty of room for dissent, and I was welcome there even though I was a Mesopagan Druid primarily interested in old-fashioned occultism. By the time I stopped going -- 2010 was my last year -- it was full of shrill political bickering and all the features that interested me had been squeezed out by the wokesters. My advice for now? Turn your back and walk away.  Once the scene finishes crashing to ruin, the rubble stops bouncing, and the woke brigade goes marching off to find some other subculture to ruin, it may be possible to put something together that will be less septic, but for the time being it's like walking into a burning building.

comments.title: Fortune's death
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I thought I would follow up here instead of on the other thread, to minimize your work going back and forth (alas, apparently not asking dumb questions is not the even lower work alternative I chose 😉).

On the August 3 MM, you said - I'll paraphrase - that you suspected her death was sacrificial in nature, she and others offered body and soul to her magical battle efforts, and the offering was accepted.

What is interesting/perturbing to me is that I misremembered it as that it was somehow an artefact of her Christianity.

I think - aside from the weird sexual hangups - that a big part of my rejection of the Christian message I'd heard was that I cannot get my head around the idea of making an innocent person die for others as a) good or b) effective at removing the murderers sin.

It seems like oceans of blood came from the idea that it was Jesus' death that should be reenacted rather than his life to really get into heaven. That the symbol of ultimate love for something, or to spread the message, or protect it, is to die for it.

I've also always loathed the war remembrance rhetoric about soldiers having died so I can speak English or what have you. Unless you're a cow I ate, you died to serve something else entirely.

So I thought, Fortune must have still had the idea she should martyr herself, if her death was a sacrificial act.

This disconcerts me, since I obviously think this is a big categorical error... But Fortune was much better educated than me on these things in all respects.

Which sucks, because I have a major martyrdom complex, and I finally thought I'd kicked the habit of letting other people make me die for their sins by realizing that sort of thing only makes the underlying problem worse. And made me neglect the people who benefit much more from me alive.

I offer my gifts to benefit my descendants every day, I rather hope I'm worth more alive than dead to them!

But I have been wondering, given that Britain was under attack, if it wasn't that she died early, but rather that she and others were given more time to serve, and died another way later than they otherwise would have, instead of being blown apart in an air raid.

Humans choose how to live, and to meet deaths they can't change with dignity, but gods do the choosing on when to die. It seems someone like Fortune would have been worth much more to the gods alive... Did Fortune sacrifice her life, or her death?

replies.title: Re: It's a puzzlement!
replies.content: One of the things I've noticed with divination methods is that it's very often necessary to calibrate their meanings to fit your circumstances. If you live the kind of life where serious disasters happen, then the cards will tell you when you have serious disasters coming; if you live a quiet life where spilling a carton of milk is about the worst that's likely to happen to you in a day, then that's what it'll talk about. This is why it helps to keep detailed notes on your readings and to go back over each reading once you know how things turned out -- you get a better sense of what each reading was saying to you.

replies.title: Re: Arthurian Myths
replies.content:
The Arthurian legends are a story of failure, and people who identify themselves too closely to the main figures and incidents in the core legends replicate that failure right down to the fine details. I've long since lost track of how many magical orders I've seen that used Arthurian legends as their core symbolism, and proceeded to blow themselves apart in internecine political struggles of the sort that brought down Camelot.

There are, however, at least two legends that have gotten caught up in the Arthurian mythos that aren't necessarily subject to the same rule. The first is the Grail legend; the second is the legend of the life of Merlin. Those were originally independent stories, and still have their own internal dynamic.

replies.title: Re: Numerology in the Book of Druidry
replies.content: The material in Druid Revival sources on magical number theory comes almost entirely from older occult writings. You might try K.S. Guthrie's The Pythagorean Sourcebook and Library for one good collection of source material, and W.W. Westcott's Numbers, Their Occult Power and Mystic Virtues (conveniently online here) for another. Mr Greer,

Long time reader but only after a crazy incident bookending an intense two months do I feel compelled to ask you something.

I matched and met this girl on 5th Sept (Mars RX start) and we felt wildly in love, the most intense thing I've ever experienced, I became a poet, words flowed out of me, energy to do things I never had; she had incredible effect on me. I similarly had an electrifying effect on her, and we discussed marriage, getting engaged in dec, etc. Electrifiying sex that I haven't had in a decade etc.

She could read my moods, and understood my brain better than anyone else had in the country I live in. She was religious and spiritual, poured water to the sun god everyday (and started doing it for me) etc. I felt a spiritual love for someone I'd never done.

Yet, I was skeptical something could be so good and perfect and so fast, so I wrote 10 pages of notes on her the whole time to keep track. There were inconsistencies and questions I had about some things I'd noticed (her selfishness, extreme need to get growth out of people around her, some other issues) that I wondered what they were about.

Then, on 6th Oct, after 2 great days spent together discussing all of this including marriage, I dropped her at the airport for a work trip where she was meeting a colleague from Germany from her company, she cries at the airport, tells me she loves me, we'll make it all happen.

Then she ghosts me, no message nothing. Oct incredibly painful month, I cry even though I never cry, stress through the roof, everything else falling apart in my life. I pray everyday to find a way. She sends 1 email telling me she's in a crisis but will be back.
Email as confusing and something felt off.

But I kept away to give her space.

Then yesterday after I had someone read our marriage chart—who told me it was a good chart and we had a shot at a successful love marriage—I mustered the energy to message her telling her I'm not giving up not leaving, she is my wife until proven otherwise.

She finally cracked in the morning asking me "what are you doing to me", "I still think of you", then dropped the bomb:
1) She was in a relationship the whole time with someone! She never said anything to either of us.
2) He came down to see her and now they're getting married!
3) Her colleague was her boss, who was the boyfriend. She worked for her boyfriend's company!
4) She fabricated her personality and completely used me for emotional stimulation while she was here.
5) She still wanted me to fight for her after all of this! She wanted me to convince her to marry me.

I calmly said I'm happy for her and she should get married to him. She got really mad at my response. Asked if I were jealous? I said I never thing of others so jealousy/envy has never been my vice. This made her mad.

She asked for a "grander goodbye" when she was back, but I calmly said while I have loved you, I can't absolve you of your guilt and shame, and your karma is yours to bear. I walked out.

My body had a flush and I felt relieved. Suddenly everything clicked and I realised I was entranced by an emotional abuser and someone with deep personality imbalances.

My therapist who is an astrologer and mystic and can read energy really well warned me that this woman was emotionally unhinged but I tried to ignore that message, but it was completely true.

I'm only glad this ended so fast because this wouldn't destroyed me if this went on longer. And I believe she would've carried this charade for months/years to get what she was getting out of it.

Mr Greer, apologies for the wall of text, but as of 2nd Nov now that it is over, and lasted less than 2 months (1 incredible month, 1 pure suffering).
1) was this karmic? because it felt like meeting her was fate. She lives in NYC but got stuck in India for 6 months due to COVID and lives in a part of town that I would never go to. Something drew me there.
2) All of this felt completely out of my control, like the process was its own thing. I felt no agency.
3) Anything astrological or energetic you can tell me about this?
4) How do I heal fast? My system feels a massive shock, like I escaped an abuser.
5) Felt like an anima experience. All kinds of weird things were happening to me. Trying to understand what to make of it?
6) Do charts not tell you if someone is unstable? My therapist called it just from her photo and her emails, but my therapist is gifted that way.

comments.title: Going slow
comments.content:
I've been wondering if it's okay to go very slowly, and if it's okay/safe to only do the SOP opening, for example, perhaps for the rest of this lifetime.

Trying to move further along the druid path has been difficult, and promoted backward-steps, such as reading Jung and self-help books specific to my recovery from emotional abuse during childhood,to hopefully break past the resistance to journaling. I did divination for a couple of months, and became upset by repeated readings about my "failure to learn from past experiences," whether positive or negative. So I stopped a few weeks ago.

I wonder if magical initiation is perhaps not a project I should work on in this incarnation, and how to distinguish between resistance that I should just try to push forward through with metaphorically gritted teeth, and resistance that's trying to tell me another path is more appropriate and would unfold more smoothly. Due to the child abuse, I do not know myself well at all.

In the meantime, if all I can manage (without guilt-shame-depression from self-imposed pressure) to consistently do is the SOP opening, to seek a little bit of protection and stability and lessen the general crud, is that okay?

replies.title: Re: Going slow
replies.content: It's always okay to take it slow. There are no prizes for getting things done in a hurry -- quite the contrary, slow and steady is the way to succeed in magic. It may be that just doing that simple opening ritual is what's right for you in this incarnation -- and if that's the case, doing it will benefit you, and prepare you to go further in a future life.

comments.title: Is the consumer spell fading?
comments.content:
I think the spell may have finally lost its hold over me. I've returned to Manhattan in order to vote tomorrow, and the weird consumption hyping that we call retail, brought to such a grotesquely distorted art on this island, seems like some archaic hold-over from a distant age. It's positively surreal! All those debt-financed, cutting-edge, team-designed marketing makeovers gone stale overnight and beginning to smell off. Is it just me, or is the whole concept of consumerism, so cynically pushed throughout my life, finally beginning its inexorable and much-deserved descent toward the compost pile?

The yuppie gym chain Equinox branded itself as so elite that non-members can't even look inside. Most gyms use big plate glass windows to flaunt their customers' sweating out at the street. Equinox instead uses a hotel-concierge-like desk and a sports apparel shop as its street lure (exclusivity is apparently the real lure.) Now don't those ultra-modern, space-age-lighted, overpriced clothing peddlers just look tragic and desperate at this point? How could such ridiculous status signaling have managed to successfully imply hipness and polish less than a year ago? So much has changed so quickly.

Most overpriced brands communicate nothing but deceptive marketing practices to me now. Fifth Avenue, with all its prestige-brand stores shuttered and barricaded for the impending riots, gives off a painfully out-of-touch vibe. "If we can just hold on until this temporary chaos passes, happy days will be here again! Won't they? Please, someone tell us the party ain't over!" Whatever kind of hopey-change they're smoking, it must be really strong to blot out awareness of the deep restructuring of priorities that I'm feeling sweep across the land. How could the consumerist, keeping-up-with-the-Joneses spell have held sway for so long? And broken so quickly? Or am I calling the inflection point early? I hope not because it is such a draining kind of magic.

I walked down to Trump tower to find out whether the security had been beefed up for any election antics (yes it has), only to discover that a crane, building one of tallest and ugliest new apartment prisons for the elite, started raining debris on 57th Street last night. "Clutch the pearls! Not our 57th Street, dear!" "Dahling, don't worry yourself! A few deplorable slaves had to die building the pyramids too, you know." Not a pretty picture the day before an election pitting populism against the elites - Kek has a peculiar sense of humor. Kek, kek, kek.

Almost as horrifying was the sweater on the mannequin in the window of the Gucci store in Trump Tower. It has very convincing moth holes knitted into it, for that perfect shabby squalor look. Either I am totally off base, or these fools are so far gone in their Petit-Trianon masquerades that they can no longer tell the grim reaper from an invited guest...

- Christophe

replies.title: Re: Is the consumer spell fading?
replies.content: That's fascinating. I somehow managed to dodge the whole consumer-psychology business -- shopping for me is a chore, not a pleasure, and I prefer cheap and practical in every context -- but it'll be interesting to find out if other people are also noting the bloom has gone off the plastic rose...

replies.title: Re: Is the consumer spell fading?
replies.content:
A fine piece of writing, Christophe. I could visualize the desperate pearl-clutching 5th Avenue elites in your essay.

I sense the same "bloom is off the rose" vibe whenever I drive past car dealerships filled with shiny, overpriced, tech-heavy sport utility vehicles, the dealerships' lots noticeably devoid of shoppers. I see the flashy new Tesla cars around town and get the same hollow, unfulfilled-promise-of-progress vibe. The wheel rims are already showing corrosion.

I feel the malign enchantment of consumerism beginning to unravel.

replies.title: Re: Is the consumer spell fading?
replies.content: Consumerism is a material way to try to fill a spiritual void...no wonder it never works.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm currently in an MA and also working through LRM. I do much reading for my MA that I lack time to properly unpack anything, since even skimming sometimes leaves me struggling to get through everything I'm supposed to read. This is one of the many reasons I have no interest in pursuing a career in academia, and wouldn't be here at all if it weren't for the shutdown making this one of the only ways I could get an income: the habits of thought are such that actually getting deep into material is impossible.

However, I'm also working through LRM, and I've realized that the kind of reading which I'm learning to do in order to handle academia is actively detrimental for being able to make sense of occult texts. This is true even for those which are comparatively very clear!

To some extent, the exercise of focusing on a sentence a day is going to help me learn the proper way to read occult texts; the habit of reading once, then rereading and picking the seven most important sentences forces me to break the bad habit which my degree is forcing upon me. Are there other things you can think of to help with learning to dive deeply into a text for meaning, or at least, to avoid getting worse at this task while I make arrangements to bail on academia?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Another habit I find very useful is that of reading the text I'm going to meditate on before I go to bed, and meditating on it the next morning. Sleeping on it allows the unconscious to get to work on the text, and to feed insights into the conscious mind.

It used to be that a college student would spend a semester working with a single book. People came out of colleges with a much better education in those days...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One of the reasons I considered it at all was that I had an excellent professor: in one class we spent an hour talking about what could be gleamed by a single sentence of a text. We never had to read more than a few pages a week for his classes, and I learned more, and had more stick, from them than I did in all my other ones put together, so I find it easy to believe education was much better with more time spent on a single book....

More broadly, I'm discovering that I got very lucky with my undergraduate degree, and things are far more toxic than I thought....

comments.title: Practice for Election Day
comments.content:
I’m planning to do some practice this evening and on election day. I’ve had a Buddhist shrine for many years. Right now, as I’m setting it up for this practice, I’m adding a small Statue of Liberty, a United States flag, and a doll’s house Post Office mailbox.

It seems appropriate to bring something from the world of Nature. I have sage, lavender, and cedar from my garden. Also Michaelmas Daisy flowers, from a plant I’ve grown for more than 50 years (transplanting it to new locations when I move.) I also have a small slab of agate rock.

This is the first time I’ve ever added special objects to my shrine – I learned this from ecosophia. Thank you!!

- - - -
I’ll be doing Buddhist practices for purification and auspicious circumstances. I’ve also written a prayer for this moment.

For the United States election: may it be free and fair, peaceful and decisive. May all participants be blessed with clarity, wisdom and compassion.

After the election, may those who are surprised or disturbed by the results find balance and healing. May all those living within the United States be blessed. May our ability to listen and hear each other increase.

Whatever merit there may be in this practice, I dedicate it for the benefit of all sentient beings.

- - - -
JMG, you’ve been an important part of my life since 2009. Thank you for all your work!!!
EllenZ

comments.title: Thanks
comments.content:
I just finished learning the last section in the Sphere of Protection, and PRESTO! A lot of my feelings of fear and inadequacy that I associate with writing are gone! I just jumped right in, wrote the scene I wanted to, and stopped without any exhaustion or dumb self defeating emotions for 3 days in a row! I also felt a palpable change around my home, and on my feet and hands; similar to a really, really good Qigong session. They're both hot and slightly uncomfortable, with a sort of sheath of pressure around them that shifts and changes slightly over time. My place feels nice and peaceful, like a fresh wind blew through it. JMG, I wanted to thank you sincerely for your helpful instructions with the Sphere; its a nice practice that feels somehow less of a commitment than Qigong. I can also do daily without my ADHD brain getting in the way, which is excellent.

For JMG and the rest of the magickal wonderfolk, I have a couple of questions.
1. I have been doing the Sphere deity-less, but I've been thinking of adding in some ancient Iranian/Zoroastrian deities that I have been studying and reading about for funsies (I was thinking Ahura Mazda, Mithra, Anahita, and Spenta Armaiti, maybe adding Haurvetat and Vohu Mana). Does anyone have any experience with this pantheon? Also, if I invoke them in the Sphere, do I need to worship and give offerings?
2. I have felt a strange compulsion when practicing the SOP; I feel guilty if I do not bow and thank the powers a second time, after doing. Does this mess with the ... rhythm of the practice, for lack of a better term?

Much thanks!
(ps, I'm Derpherder, I've commented on the main blog a couple of times, I'm going to make a Dreamwidth account to keep up with magic mondays, but I'm too lazy and scatterbrained rn!)

replies.title: Re: Thanks
replies.content:
1) Hmm! You don't need to worship and do offerings, but Zoroastrian deities are apparently picky about morality, so don't invoke them unless you're comfortable with at least the basics of the Zoroastrian moral code.

2) You can certainly do that. I always thank the powers for their gifts, though I don't bow.

replies.title: Re: Thanks
replies.content:
Very interesting! That makes a great deal of sense, as Zoroastrianism seems to be one of the great transition points in human religiosity, towards a mixture of nature reverence and abstract moralism. I always liked Zoroastrianism because it didn't take that central idea and go crazy with it like Christianity and Islam. The love for the elements in Zoroastrianism is palpable, which I guess makes sense given the common Indo-European roots between it and Celtic Druidism!

I'll have to think deeply about whether I want to get involved with strict, structured and semi-rigid morality. Every time I've done that--politically, spiritually, or religiously--I've had my hyperactive mind find some flaw in it, and pick it apart until I could not longer abide living by it. I am very comfortable with the Zoroastrian injunctions towards good thoughts, good words, and good deeds, but the purity laws are really something else. I actually read in one book, that a theory for why some people converted from Zoroastrianism to Islam in Iran was because of the onerousness of dealing with ever present priestly regulations around spiritual pollution!

Thanks for your help, JMG. Have a nice election night, I assume you'll be chuckling all the way home from the bank!
